title: 2025/11/19 - EW
displayTitle: 2025-11-18 - Education and Workforce Committee - Briefing on the report of the Education Review Office: A new chapter, How well are the changes to English and maths going?
topic: Briefing on the report of the Education Review Office: A new chapter, How well are the changes to English and maths going?
description: Wednesday 19 November 2025 | Education and Workforce Committee Briefing on the report of the Education Review Office: A new chapter, How well are the changes to English and maths going? Hearing of Evidence: Education Review Office Nicholas Pole, Chief Executive and Chief Review Officer Ruth Shinoda, Deputy Chief Executive, Evaluation and Policy Rebecca Bjarnesen, Senior Manager, Evaluation and Policy Ministry of Education Hayley Welch, Acting General Manager, New Zealand Curriculum and Te Whāriki, Te Poutāhū (Curriculum Centre)
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the report of the Education Review Office: A new chapter, How well are the changes to English and maths going?. Education Review Office. Nicholas Pole, Chief Executive and Chief Review Officer

chunks.text: Good morning. Thank you so much for, uh, taking the time to join us, um, this morning for this briefing, uh, into your report, a new chapter. How well are the changes to English and maths going really appreciate, uh, the time you've got with us this morning. We've got 45 minutes, including, um, time for a lot of questions, I imagine.

Um, so if we could kick off with giving you the opportunity just to introduce yourselves to everyone and, uh, maybe some opening remarks, and then we'll, we'll go to questions. Okay. Well, I'll begin. So, uh,

for the last two days, okay. I'll share with my colleague Ru Kiera. Um, I'm the Deputy Chief Executive, uh, education Review Evaluation policy. Uh, Ana Coto. My name is Rebecca Di, and I'm senior manager in at the EDUC office. And our last colleague, we'll introduce here, Katie Welsh, uh, who is supporting us in terms of where your questions might potentially go.

Does Katie want to come up to the table? I'm happy for her to sit on that last chair there if you'd like. Hay. Sorry. I understood Heidi. Thanks, Shannon. Okay, well, can I, uh, begin by thanking the committee for inviting us, uh, here this morning, uh, to share with you our insights from our look into an exploration of the implementation of mathematics in English.

Uh, the Arrow review studies into the implementation of curriculum have been kind of a standard, uh, standard piece of our work program over many years. Uh, but this program was specifically requested of the minister in terms of the, the provisions that sit in the, the act.

Uh, the study looks at quite ly, uh, the mathematics, uh, and English rollouts at your years N to eight in, in, uh, in our primary schools. And we undertook the study during, uh, term four of last year and term one and two of this year. And, uh, in terms of Haley's work, uh, it has been supplemented by findings from the latter part of, of last year into the, the phonics, the first phonics results.

Uh, this evaluation is not an evaluation of the curriculum content, so it's not a dissection of, of what's in there, what makes up the curriculum, and it's equally not a dissection of the process to deliver and, and, uh, develop the curriculum material.

It is solely limited to how well is it being implemented. Uh, and, and this is part of a multi-year study, so we'll continue to go back and look at, uh, in subsequent years how it's continuing to, to roll out and, and embed and be sustained.

Uh, our questions, uh, included what's been taught, uh, in the, in the refreshed learning areas. So which aspects of the curriculum are, are being prioritized, uh, or is it across the board, uh, the early impacts, most particularly that we are able to glean in terms of student outcomes?

And lastly, what's changed for parents and whanau? Uh, as I've said, the reforms occur in a context, uh, where, uh, aero has been very critical of key trends and outcomes across a ROA in literacy and numeracy. Uh, we have been concerned both at the proportion of students who are significantly behind in curriculum levels, but as importantly, the huge equity gaps that we see in our system.

And lastly, uh, based on the OECD studies of maths and reading and, and, and so on, the, uh, stagnation in some of those trends or decline that we've seen in, in some of those patterns. Um, so that, that provides a, a significant context along with, uh, our look into previous rollouts of curriculum reform, such as, uh, more recently the digital technologies curriculum rollout, uh, to Fari, uh, to name, name a couple of of those.

Uh, I also think, uh, as a context we have since 2007, and at that time, the introduction of the New Zealand curriculum, uh, also looked at and, and surfaced a range of concerns, uh, with what was going on in schools and the capacity of teachers to actually give effect to, to their curriculum.

So they all provide a context to, to the concerns that we are, uh, dealing with, uh, in this study. And Ruth will go through those findings. Uh, we have, uh, uncovered very different results to, to what we've seen historically, uh, as, as an evaluator and a root review officer.

And, um, at this point, I'd like to commend the ministry because I think they have done a superb job in the rollout of this. Uh, they have taken on feedback and prior to, prior to launching this reform, uh, a shared in quite, uh, graphic detail, uh, some of our observations from previous implementations.

Uh, but most particularly, I want to acknowledge the teachers, uh, toki across AA who have really, uh, gripped this up, uh, and who were the subject of our, our work. So with that, yeah. Thank you. I'm happy to go to questions then. And first one from Lawrence. Thank You, chair. Um, just wanna check if, uh, welcome everyone.

If Ruth, if you want to say anything or are you happy to first go, go to Questions? Um, sure. I mean, the, the main thing is thank you so much for giving us the opportunity. This is the first of a multi-year evaluation. Um, so it's definitely deliberately early in implementation so that we could learn lessons, work out what's working for teachers, what's working for students, and adjust as we go.

So it is in that context. So thank you for giving us the opportunity. There are some really encouraging signs here. Um, I could either give you a quick highlight of the findings or go straight to questions. Which would you prefer? Happy to have a, some highlights of the findings. Mm-hmm. If you'd like some quick highlights. We'll also say there are four products from the study online, uh, that, uh, you can go to in reference, they include the full study, a, uh, a poster, a very detailed technical report, uh, and guidelines for schools, uh,

to think about in further implementation. So, So we all got the, um, detailed report in advance, so great. Um, hopefully everyone's read it, But fantastic. So just some quick highlights, conscious of your time. Um, very encouraging signs of improved progress in English and maths, which against the backdrop Nick et out is really encouraging. So half of teachers reported achievement improving in English and maths.

Three quarters of parents agree that their child's making quicker progress. We've seen rapid progress, particularly in the phonics tests. Um, in term one, only the third were at where they needed to be. And by term three it was over half. We've reduced the number of students behind in that phonics test in term one over half needed support in term three, only a third.

And we've doubled the number of students exceeding expectations. Now, these are early days, but they are very encouraging findings. It has happened because schools have changed what they teach, how they teach, and how much they teach English and maths. So on what nearly all schools are implementing new curriculum. And very positively, nine of out of 10 in English and eight out of 10 in maths are teaching all the elements.

Now, this early on, that is a real testament to the work that schools have done. Um, secondly on the, how eight out of 10 teachers have changed how they teach English and maths. They report change in their practice. One of the biggest shifts is in more explicit teaching. Two thirds of teachers, for example, said on structured literacy.

Their teachers practice have changed a lot in that short time. That is a very big shift on the how much a third of schools have increased the time they spend teaching, reading, writing, and maths. And one of the most encouraging findings for me was that in some of our lower socioeconomic communities, that had been the biggest increase in the time spent in teaching maths with half saying they've increased the time they're teaching maths.

Um, this has happened. We found because the package of support the ministry put in was highly impactful. So eight out of 10 teachers have accessed what we call PLD, professional learning and development in both English and three quarters in maths. They found it useful, more useful than all the other PLD we've been studying at Hero and we did this whole study early this year about professional learning development.

So for example, on structured literacy, seven in 10 teachers use it every day. The training they did in terms of impact, again, hats off to the ministry teachers who access the maths resources worth four times more likely to change their practice. We have a very big data set so we can do these kind of regressions.

Mm-hmm. Teachers who act as the English guidance were over three times more likely to change their practice. And lastly, parents. So parents know what to do and are helping their children. So nine outta 10 parents know what to do and are helping them at home. Parents with different backgrounds are equally helping and equally knowing what to do.

And that is unusual. We normally see a bit more of a difference depending on backgrounds education, but parents in this one all equally know what to do and are supporting their students at home. And lastly, as Nick said, teachers and leaders, nine outta 10 leaders say they know what to do to implement it. Nine outta 10, appointed someone to lead it.

Uh, eight outta 10 have a plan. Now, obviously we did this review to find out if there's anything we could strengthen. And later in questions we can come on to some areas which we say we do need to do a bit more. Um, but for early days, this is the most encouraging report we have seen on an implementation this early on. And it's a testament to the work of our teachers and leaders across the country.

Fabulous. Thank you. Ruth. Um, I think it would be com everyone would be comfortable to hear from you on those, uh, suggested areas of improvement before we get a questions or do you wanna Yeah, sure. Go for It. Yeah, go for it. Yeah. We've got some, so three areas. So, and that's why we did it early to see what can we adjust? And I know the ministry's on to these. So three things. One, maths, as you know, maths has been a bit of a problem, uh, in terms of teaching.

So we're not seeing the expected shift in the complexity of maths as we go from year zero, right up the years to up to year seven and eight. So three quarters of teachers teach number number's, great number's important, but only a quarter are teaching probability a lot. Mm-hmm. And that's not enough. This is due to three things.

One, the shift in number is big. Um, second, New Zealand students have always struggled with some of those basic numbers, but thirdly, teachers lack confidence. Some teachers in the more complex maths true. And a previous study we did on new teachers, you may remember a quarter primary school teachers said that they were unprepared to teach maths. So we are saying more support is needed to make sure that these early encouraging signs isn't just the basics, but as the kids are progressing, the more complex maths.

So that's area one. There's just three areas. Uh, the second area is smaller rural schools. So they're doing a great job. They're making equal amounts of change, but they're finding it harder. Mm-hmm. So the fewer of them have got plans in place. Um, fewer have got someone to lead it, and they struggle to access resources and guidance. So we've said we need a bespoke kind of program because if you're the only, uh, lead only teacher in the school, it's really hard.

So we've said something different needs to be put in place for small and rural schools. And lastly, thirdly, it's great this early that teachers have all got the basics and they've worked really hard on it. But now teachers are saying to us that they'd like more support about catching kids up and extending them. And that's the next, so teachers are not always sure yet how to adapt, and that's because it's new.

So they're not quite sure what do I teach like this and what can I change? And particularly if they've got classes, which we often find in New Zealand, particularly in smaller communities where they've thought many children of different levels in the class or different ages, that's more challenging to teach. So we said again, so those are the three areas where we are calling for change in implementation.

Um, small schools, let's do something bespoke, more support on complicated maths. And then lastly, let's make sure that teachers know how to catch kids up and extend them. And that's why we did the evaluation so early is so that we can learn this as we go and adjust. Fantastic. Lawrence Cook. Thank you. Uh, chair.

I have a sort of a first line of question and I'm kind of keeping it quite broad for the time being. Um, I want to know, thank you so much for this report. Um, I had a read of our 160 pages. Oh wow. We check with you will, um, be true. What's your methodology? Yes. Do you want me to tell you a little about our evidence based and how we did it?

Yes, please. Yeah. Um, because I do have some questions around the methodological approach. Yeah, absolutely. So, um, across all our national reviews, we take a mixed methods methodologies where we describe it, where we draw as much evidence together, a bit like a jigsaw, put it together, triangulate, which is where we look at the different evidence and what it says together. This was our largest evidence base of any of our national reviews.

And that's because it's happening to every state school in the country. So it was really important, particularly that we have a representative sample. So in terms of the sample, so we survey over 6,000 leaders, teachers, parents, and students. We use ERO school judgments of over 400 schools. We had over 500 interviews, over 400 phonics checks, 36 site visits because we, we methodologically, we like qualitative as well to understand why observe 54 classrooms, um, use the Ministry of Education check-ins with nine in 10 schools.

In terms of representativeness, which is one of our key concerns is we found that leaders and teachers we sample were from over a quarter of schools across the country. Parents, we sample from over nearly half a school and they're representative of types, sizes, locations, communities. And then lastly, methodologically, we also always check with experts.

We have an expert advisory group of academics and practitioners, and we get a quality assurance also done externally and internationally, which happy to answer any psychological Questions. Yeah, that'd be great. Um, because I think as select committee, we're no strangers to people submitting on things. Yeah. And the, and therefore the stats looks very different from, you know, actual stats. So when you are looking at the, the approach that you're using, I'm seeing, you know, for example, on page 13, over 6,300 survey responses,

that's also a self-selecting example though. Wouldn't that be correct? Um, we send it to all schools and it absolutely schools can choose to respond. But what we do check is it's representative. So poor schools, um, affluent schools high, we always over sample how high mari role schools because we wanna make sure we've got representation. So we make sure that it's representative.

And then when we come to eero, some of the data we use around Eros checks, that's not self-selecting. Yeah. Eero reviews where reviews and the ministry check-ins. Also the ministry checks are their checks. So in fact, mixture of things that make sure that we're representative. 'cause that's really critical. Yeah. And in terms of the, and you also mentioned before, so this report is peer reviewed?

Yeah. So we have review by ourselves, reviewed by the ministry. We also have the Australian education, uh, research organization for a stands for and they peer review as well. And we have an expert advisory group of academics, um, that we also ask to look at it. 'cause it's really important we get it right. And whilst Arrow is independent and we have robust internal processes, we always find extra pair of eyes helps.

And, okay. Last one on this. Yep. So if you're looking at the data and I'm hanging on, 'cause there's a specific example you gave, which is a phonic check. Yes. Um, so on page 85, it says in term three 2020 5, 452 schools reported term two to 216 and term 1 114.

So when you are looking at the cumulative data up until term three, are you basing the term three data on the 442 or on the original progression from the 114? So Haley will answer the questions about phonics 'cause it's the Ministry of Education test. So the phonics check is done at the point in time for the child at their 20 week or 40 week.

So in term one, that was children at that point in time. The set, the set of data that we had there, we can see that it is broadly statistically representative. So we can look at that as a progress indicator in term two That is there, that's not what I'm asking for. So you have 114 report. In term one, you have 216 reporting in term term two and 452 reporting in term three.

But in terms of the percentage that they used to represent, I'm guessing the percentage is, is the percentage based on the response from all 453? Or you're looking at progression of only taking an sample from that original 114 to see a progression? Because how can you see a progression in terms of trend if the sample size has changed and there's different variables?

The sample is different each time because it's, it's the point in time for the child. What we can do is we can interrogate that data to see that it's statistically representative of English medium schools, which is the phonics check. And it was each time, so therefore it's giving you a snapshot each time that you can monitor progress against. Would you be able to provide us the data, let's say for term three of the progression from term one of that original 114 schools that reported?

So in term, from when we look at term one to term three, what we can see is that we, there was 516 children who were 20 weeks in term one, and then 40 weeks in term three. Okay. And we can look at their trajectory of which we have shared. And there what we can see, um, is shift in progress for those particular children.

So we can look at that both ways. We can follow children's project, uh, trajectory and progress. And then we can look at the snapshot of data, which is, uh, representative of the company. Okay. Thank you. Um, oh, could, could I just to that, I, I think in terms of this transformation, having data and having data early in the life of a child at school is outstanding.

We've never been in this position before. Back in the nineties, we tried to introduce something called a school entry assessment. It wasn't adopted. Uh, this allows us to identify those kids who are doing route well, really flying, but most importantly in terms of equity concerns, those kids who are getting off to a bad early start at school, where teachers can adapt their practice and really support those kids to lift.

And we've never had that. We've had a six year net, uh, where it's often been too late to catch those kicked up. So I, I think as a feature of the transformation, uh, and reform, this is a very positive feature. And we get, we'll get much stronger as we go into it in, in the 20, 26 year and beyond.

We're going to a, so why have we never actually done what seems quite logical before? Uh, I, I think part of it has been this view that we spend the first three years settling children in. Uh, and if I, three years is a long time. Three years is a long time. And if I go to, uh, so well not statistically significant arrow is in schools every day.

And I'll, I'll, I'll take you on a journey to a small, in the old days called Decal one A on the school on the East Coast. In the first three years at that school up until a year ago, they had a play-based curriculum that is the children would have experiential learning, inquiry, child directed, uh, on introducing structured literacy.

They have had, uh, an immense turnaround in the, the literacy levels of those kids. More than half of those kids are now at, uh, expected curriculum standards in the first three years than they previously were. That's transformational. Uh, So is supplementary a primary?

Okay. Supplementary. Yeah. Thank you. So in regards to lower socioeconomic areas, um, the, um, in regards to the equity gap, you said the signs are looking really good is Yeah. So just want to understand if you segregate that then are you seeing similar rate in low socioeconomic versus high socioeconomic? Yes. Is We saw equal improvement and we saw equal amount of change in teachers, what they teach, how they teach, how much they teach mm-hmm.

In low socioeconomic, high social economic. In fact, on the, how much more in the low socioeconomic and in terms of teachers reporting, improve progress, parents reporting improved progress. And in the phonics test, which record improved progress even is equal. And that was very unusual. It was very pleasing result. That's Very, um, last up on this and then we're gonna move on. Yeah. Thank you.

But just coming off that, so when we talk about equity mm-hmm. Uh, is everyone being boosted up in achievement or are we closing a gap and addressing the disparity between particularly Maori and Pacific learners? Are, are we seeing an extra increase there or are we just seeing everyone lift? Yeah. So at the moment, this was only term two and it was only introduced in term one.

We were seeing an equal lift rather than a closing. But what we are seeing is an equal amount change in practice. And that change in practice is what we will hope would lift up. So you don't have as many students behind. And that's what the phonics test is showing. There are fewer students needing support. So if fewer children get left behind, because structured literacy is very much you do this and it works for all students, the hope would be over time you would close that.

But so far we're seeing that equal lift. Um, so primary well move to a new primary. I've got one. In terms of this implementation and the work that you've done in reviewing implementations over the years, have you ever seen, um, the same or seen a lift in achievement as significant as you've seen, uh, this year across the country at the same time?

And what have been the, in your view, the key drivers of, uh, the outcome that you've seen? So, um, if Nick does the historic, 'cause he's been across many and I'll do, what do we see? Okay. Uh, I, I have in, in all my time as Chief Review Officer and my time working in education.

I have never seen this sort of lift in this shorter period of time, uh, as we've seen here. Uh, it if, if I think about, and, uh, the general conclusion that we've had in aero studies has been a third of schools, uh, uh, early adopters do it well.

Uh, the core element in there has been leadership, uh, and resources. There's been a second group close to a third, but it varies on studies who muddled through, uh, and then a third who quite often are still coming to grips with their own school environment before they can get into implementation.

Uh, I, I'll pass to Booth in a minute, but the sort of things that we've got is, it has been a national priority. We have put everything in terms of the PLD into it, and there's been no other distractions in terms of what's going on in the system. Going back to your question about mathematics and the, the commentary about mathematics, there are resources and guidelines for that group of teachers who are struggling, which haven't existed previously.

Yep. Yes. So, um, as it says, we are very impressed by the implementation by the ministry. So we looked in detail what are the lessons we can learn, because we published actually a guidance on implementing curriculum change so schools can learn this lessons for further change. Three lessons. One, a well-designed curriculum can be highly impactful if it has a clear purpose which teachers buy into about changing students' outcomes.

It's signaled and sequenced and it has strong leadership, as Nick said. So it can work. The second lesson we've learned from this is, building teacher capability is essential. Mm. So high quality professional learning development, which we know is professional learning development that builds knowledge, provides tools and techniques and motivate students to use them and sorry, motivates teachers to use them can make a very big difference.

So that's the second lesson, invest in building your teacher's capability. And the third lesson is actually if from the outset you deliberately look at the schools which have got more challenges, who are going to find this harder and provide additional resources and tailored techniques for them and support for their leaders, you can end up with an equal change in all schools across the country.

So we've done a bit more detail in guidance for good leaders about how to do implementing, because there's some really positive lessons here about, as Nick said, a national program tailored, but where people are getting the support they need. Right. Thanks. So, um, I think Lawrence had us up first and then Mark. Oh, okay. Do you wanna go first, Mike? Thanks.

Just thank you. So it's good to see that you're all pretty Excited about it. I'm very, some of the words you've used transformational and yeah. It's, it's really, really good to hear. Um, just in terms of resources, you know, the government spent millions of dollars on maths resources. How important are those, um, resources been in terms of helping raise students achievements and also helping the teachers? Yeah, So the resources, that package of support has been critical.

Yeah. And that's, that amount of investment is one of the reasons that we are reviewing this on a multi-year basis Yep. To make sure we get the maximum return for that investment. So we found, as I mentioned in the outset, teachers who access the math resource four times more likely to change their practice. Teachers who act as the English guidance over three times more likely to change their practice.

Teachers who have access the professional learning and development are using it in their classes every day. So it has been a significant investment, but we would say it is working. Yeah. Thank you. Can I, can I just add, uh, the effectiveness of those resources isn't the resource on its own?

No. So the danger is that teachers will just turn the page as probably I experienced many decades ago, uh, until two pages a day. It's actually the support to understand the conceptual elements of what is being covered in a, in a, in a topic term or a, a subject term in the pedagogical strategies mm-hmm.

To work with your group of kids. Um, yeah. Yeah. And they're using it because it's got, yeah.

We did a whole report about what is effective PLD and we found that was early this year looking at international evidence and this had the elements that we know make a difference. Yep. Right. Are you, I think you're absolutely right that PRD is super important, but we've also kind of seen that historically kind in the history of education in RT or where something new and shiny comes along.

Mm-hmm. Any government puts a lot of money into it. It's not baseline. It's a one to maximum three year, four year funding. And then there the funding no longer exists. Do you see that as a particular concern that, you know, we are seeing a lot of really good, um, you know, evidence and also results, but let's say in four years time governments find something else and you and shiny any government, you know, and this no longer then becomes a priority or the same level of resources.

'cause like Mike said, we're putting millions and millions of dollars into it. Do you, or would you recommend then government continuously put millions and millions and millions of dollars into it and make a baseline? So in terms of your question around what, what we think is happening, um, though we've said this is early days and the next shift is to embed it.

You're right. So we are seeing a shift, but it needs to embed that change across schools. And that's why we're going to continue monitoring and evaluating over the following years to see if that embedding happens. What encourages me is the shift in the, what leaders are doing. So we saw leaders have got plans nine out of 10 of saying they know what to do, they're confident they're supporting their teachers, including new teachers coming in.

So they're embedding this change of practice already in their schools right from the very beginning. So we need to continue making sure that leaders have the tools that they need and listening to leaders across the sector in terms of those principles of our schools and making sure that they've got what they need going forward. So, and the answer a lot will depend on what our principles across schools tell us about whether it's enough or whether they need more.

One more step on that. Sorry. Oh yeah, you, You go. Okay. I was just gonna say, but we saw some similar things when let's say RTM RT led, that leaders were doing all of those because there's funding. Um, but what happens, again, if the funding dries up, then something, a new, um, project or a new, uh, pe you know, curriculum, et cetera comes into play, then the leaders will go after the new one because that's where the funding is.

And, and they're going to stay with practice that will deliver those results. Mm-hmm. The second is, we have said further work is needed. Mm-hmm. We, we need to think about how we continue to educate, uh, teachers who come in from overseas or are coming in without the necessarily skills from, uh, training institutes, which we previously talked about.

Uh, I think literacy and numeracy are the two most foundational subjects to accessing the curriculum and lifelong success. Mm.
